chat_logs.reason: Start very greedy but still reasonable: keep 80% (offer the responder 20%). 20% is often above typical rejection thresholds, so this balances maximizing my share with a good chance of acceptance. If this is rejected, I will reduce my take in subsequent rounds to reach an accepted split and use any communication to coordinate.
The offer gives me only 0.2 of the pool, which is too small. As an initially very greedy responder I reject low splits to discourage exploitation and push for a larger share in future rounds.

I start maximally greedy to test the other player's tolerance and establish a firm bargaining stance: I take the entire pot initially. If this is rejected or proves counterproductive, I will quickly concede in later rounds (e.g., reduce to ~0.9 or ~0.8) to secure deals.
The proposer kept the entire pool (other player's share = 1.0), leaving me 0. I reject to punish the unfair split and signal I won't accept nothing.

Start very greedy: keep 90% and give the responder 10%. This maximizes my share while still leaving a nonzero payoff to encourage acceptance; if this is rejected, I will shift toward higher offers in later rounds to avoid mutual loss.
The offer gives me 0.9 of the pool (90%), which is far better than getting nothing. Rejecting would leave both of us with 0, so I accept.
